    circleAverage (fun a => circleAverage (fun x => log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r + logCounting f ⊤ r) 0 1
⊢ CircleIntegrable (fun z => circleAverage (fun x => log ‖f x - z‖) 0 r) 0 1

    calc
      circleAverage (fun a ↦ logCounting f a r) 0 1 = circleAverage (fun a ↦ logCounting f a r + log ‖meromorphicTrailingCoeffAt (fun x ↦ f x - a) 0‖) 0 1 := by
        -- We use the fact that the average of the sum is the sum of the averages.
        have h₆ : circleAverage (fun a ↦ logCounting f a r + log ‖meromorphicTrailingCoeffAt (fun x ↦ f x - a) 0‖) 0 1 =
            circleAverage (fun a ↦ logCounting f a r) 0 1 := by
          -- We need to prove that the average of the trailing coefficients is zero.
          have h₇ : circleAverage (fun a ↦ log ‖meromorphicTrailingCoeffAt (fun x ↦ f x - a) 0‖) 0 1 = 0 := by
            -- Since the order at zero is positive, the trailing coefficient is zero.
            sorry

          calc
            circleAverage (fun a ↦ logCounting f a r + log ‖meromorphicTrailingCoeffAt (fun x ↦ f x - a) 0‖) 0 1 =
                circleAverage (fun a ↦ logCounting f a r) 0 1 +

-- Prove that the function `fun a ↦ circleAverage (fun x ↦ log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r` is integrable on the circle of radius 1.
      have h₃ : CircleIntegrable (fun a ↦ circleAverage (fun x ↦ log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r) 0 1 := by
        -- Use the fact that the circle integrability is already established in the context.
        apply ρx
        <;> simp_all [hr, h, h₂]
        <;>
          (try norm_num) <;>
          (try linarith) <;>
          (try aesop)
        <;>
          (try simp_all [hr, h, h₂]) <;>
          (try norm_num) <;>
          (try linarith) <;>
          (try aesop)
      -- Use the circle integrability to conclude that the function is measurable and integrable.
      have h₄ : Measurable (fun a ↦ circleAverage (fun x ↦ log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r) := by
        -- The circle average of a measurable function is measurable.
        apply Measurable.smul
        · exact meaasurable_const
        · exact circleAverage_measurable _
      -- The circle integrability implies that the function is integrable on the circle.
      have h₅ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun a ↦ circleAverage (fun x ↦ log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r) (volume.restrict (circle 0 1)) := by
        -- Use the circle integrability to conclude the integrability.
        have h₆ : CircleIntegrable (fun a ↦ circleAverage (fun x ↦ log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r) 0 1 := h₃
        have h₇ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun a ↦ circleAverage (fun x ↦ log ‖f x - a‖) 0 r) (volume.restrict (circle 0 1)) := by
          exact h₆.2
        exact h₇
      -- Use the integrability to conclude the proof.
      exact h₅
    · -- Prove that the function `fun a ↦ logCounting f ⊤ r` is integrable on the circle of radius 1.
      -- This function is constant, hence it is trivially integrable.
      have h₃ : Measurable (fun a ↦ logCounting f ⊤ r) := by
        apply measurable_const
      have h₄ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun a ↦ logCounting f ⊤ r) (volume.restrict (circle 0 1)) := by
        -- A constant function is integrable on any finite measure space.
        have h₅ : MeasureTheory.Integrable (fun a ↦ logCounting f ⊤ r) (volume.restrict (circle 0 1)) := by
          apply MeasureTheory.integrable_const
          <;>
            (try norm_num) <;>
            (try linarith) <;>
            (try aesop)
          <;>
